title: U.S.-Canada Border Security: Fireside Chat with Sec. Markwayne Mullin and Hon. Gary Anandasangaree
gist: U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin and Canadian Public Safety Minister Gary Anandasangaree describe how cartel and organized-crime pressure is migrating from the U.S. southern border to the 5,400-mile northern border, prompting deeper bilateral law-enforcement integration, new Canadian legislation, and personnel and technology investment. Both frame fentanyl, illegal guns, and human smuggling as shared, increasingly transnational threats while stressing the resilient trade and people-to-people ties underpinning roughly $900 billion in annual commerce.

themes.theme: Crime displacement to the northern border

insights.claim: U.S. Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin says pressure on cartels at the southern border — including a primary border wall set to be complete from the Pacific to the Gulf by mid-2027 — is pushing illegal activity to the U.S.-Canada border, evidenced by the first cross-border tunnel found in years.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Border-security and immigration-enforcement spend is rotating north; watch for resource and contracting shifts toward the northern border and Canadian partners.
insights.quote: Because of the pressure we’re putting on the cartels, the wall that we’re being built, which will be completed – the primary wall will be complete this time next year
insights.locator: Christopher Hernandez-Roy: Thanks very much, Alex

insights.claim: Over the last year U.S. authorities apprehended enough fentanyl on the northern border to kill 17 million Americans, arrested nationals of roughly 78 different countries crossing it, and arrest a watch-listed or wanted terrorist there almost weekly.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Quantifies the escalating northern-border threat the administration is using to justify a new enforcement posture and Canadian cooperation.
insights.quote: Over the last year we’ve apprehended enough fentanyl that would kill 17 million Americans on our northern border.
insights.locator: Christopher Hernandez-Roy: Thanks very much, Alex

insights.claim: Of the 5,400-mile U.S.-Canada border, less than 300 miles is patrolled on a daily basis, which Mullin frames as a vast territory where displaced criminal activity can take hold.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: The enforcement gap defines the scale of the surveillance, drone, and personnel investment opportunity on the northern border.
insights.quote: When you have 5,400 miles, with only less than 300 of it that’s being patrolled on a daily basis, it’s a tremendous amount of territory
insights.locator: And so the cooperation between us and Canada

insights.claim: Mullin argues not all 5,400 miles need securing because the cartels' biggest profit is human smuggling rather than drugs, and many remote stretches are too costly in infrastructure to be profitable for them.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Enforcement and detection investment will concentrate on profitable corridors with landable air/drone assets, not uniform coverage — shaping where security tech is deployed.
insights.quote: the biggest profit isn’t necessarily drugs as much as it’s human smuggling.
insights.locator: We know what’s profitable or not profitable

insights.claim: Canada's December 2024 border plan, implemented over roughly 18 months, has produced a 99 percent reduction in irregular migration from north to south along with declines in drugs and other illicit items, according to Minister Anandasangaree.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Canada is signaling measurable border progress to counter U.S. pressure and tariff/trade leverage tied to border security.
insights.quote: in fact, 99 percent reduction in irregular migration from what we have been monitoring.
insights.locator: From a strategic point of view

insights.claim: Canada is investing in 1,000 new CBSA and RCMP personnel spread across the border plus added technology including drones and Blackhawk helicopters to strengthen border security.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Concrete Canadian procurement signal — aerial and surveillance hardware plus staffing — for defense and security suppliers.
insights.quote: we’re investing on a thousand new CBSA and RCMP personnel, many of them who will be spread across the border

insights.claim: Mullin argues the core operational fix is embedding personnel from each country with the other so that intelligence can be acted on in hours rather than days or weeks, since separate verification through judicial-warrant processes otherwise lets targets move.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Operational integration, not just policy alignment, is the stated bottleneck — implies more joint operations centers and cross-deputization.
insights.quote: the asset that we’re going after – be it HUMINT or property or drugs – is moved by then.
insights.locator: But the biggest issue that we’ve really got to work on

insights.claim: Canada is aligning its laws with Five Eyes partners via Bill C-22 (lawful access), currently before parliament, to enable warrants and production orders for increasingly online and device-based cartel and organized-crime activity.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Pending Canadian surveillance legislation expands lawful access to electronic service providers — a regulatory shift relevant to tech and telecom compliance.
insights.quote: parliament’s debating a bill called Bill C-22, which is lawful access
insights.locator: On the Canadian side we are aligning

insights.claim: Anandasangaree says fentanyl deaths in Canada have fallen from about 20 per day last year to about 15 per day, while warning the drug is now creeping into Europe as markets close elsewhere, a point raised with G-7 partners.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Fentanyl demand is geographically mobile; a closing North American market implies displacement risk into Europe, a forward indicator for EU drug policy.
insights.quote: Last year, I would say there was about 20 deaths per day in Canada involving fentanyl... The numbers have gone down to about 15 per day.
insights.locator: I will say fentanyl, but we know fentanyl

insights.claim: Anandasangaree states fentanyl is not flowing north-south or south-north but arrives from overseas — naming China and India — as precursors that local dealers manufacture and distribute.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Reframes the fentanyl problem as a precursor-supply issue centered on China and India, not the U.S.-Canada border itself — relevant to U.S. tariff and diplomatic pressure narratives.
insights.quote: we know fentanyl comes from countries such as China and India.
insights.locator: I will say fentanyl, but we know fentanyl

insights.claim: Mullin says the U.S. produces little fentanyl domestically because it eliminated access to precursors, and identifies precursor control as a key area for cooperation with both northern and southern neighbors.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Precursor interdiction is the framed lever for fentanyl supply reduction — points to where regulatory and chemical-import controls will tighten.
insights.quote: one of the reasons why we don’t make a tremendous amount of fentanyl... is because we got rid of the precursors.
insights.locator: Let me just talk about the fentanyl

insights.claim: Both officials flag a postal loophole — small low-weight packages (Anandasangaree cites a 500-milligram threshold in Canada) that ship automatically without inspection — as a channel cartels exploit by sending many small parcels, with Canada committing in budget 2025 to legislation closing it.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: A specific cross-border parcel-screening regulatory change is coming — relevant to postal, e-commerce logistics, and customs-inspection operations.
insights.quote: there’s a certain size of packages that flow automatically from Canada to the United States that don’t get checked
insights.locator: The – when you talked about the fentanyl, the precursors

insights.claim: Mullin links rising organized crime in Canada to rising gun demand, arguing you cannot fight illegal guns without fighting the underlying criminal activity, citing a past U.S.-Canada partnership against biker gangs that also cut gun flows.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Frames illegal guns as a derivative of organized-crime demand, shaping where joint enforcement effort is directed in Canadian inner cities like Toronto.
insights.quote: As long as there’s a demand for organized crime up north, the gun activity is going to increase.
insights.locator: Let me just talk about the fentanyl and about the guns too

insights.claim: Anandasangaree cites roughly $900 billion in annual U.S.-Canada trade, mostly orderly, with the vast majority of Canadians living within one hour of the border, framing the relationship as deeply interdependent on both security and commerce.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Anchors the trade stakes that make border friction economically consequential; both governments are motivated to resolve 'irritations' to protect commerce.
insights.quote: Nine hundred million dollars of trade – billion dollars of trade takes place each and every year.
insights.locator: Minister Gary Anandasangaree: Well, thank you

insights.claim: Both officials repeatedly emphasize that current frictions in the relationship are 'negligible' relative to shared interests and must be worked through, signaling intent to stabilize the partnership rather than escalate disputes.
insights.speaker: CSIS
insights.so_what: Despite political tension, both sides publicly commit to de-escalation — a constructive signal for the trade and security trajectory.
insights.quote: What differences we have is negligible compared to what we have in common and the work that we’re doing together.
